tagline: Same instincts. Ten times the intel.

round_of_64.reasoning: 43.1 AdjEM gap is insurmountable. Auburn's archetype (2024 Purdue, Runner-Up) confirms elite ceiling. Alabama State's best twin (2012 Norfolk State) pulled a 15-over-2 upset — wrong seed line to project here.

Only 2.3 AdjEM gap favoring Louisville. Creighton's top archetype is 2014 Connecticut (Champion, 0.932 similarity) — that UConn team was similarly underseeded and went on a title run. Louisville's twin (2018 Michigan, Runner-Up) is strong, but Creighton's offensive efficiency (AdjO 118.4) at a controlled tempo gives them the edge in this coin-flip.

Michigan's 6.3 AdjEM edge holds. Their archetype (2014 Michigan State, Final Four) suggests deep tournament capacity. UC San Diego's best twin (2010 Tennessee, Sweet 16) reached the second weekend, making this closer than pure efficiency suggests, but Michigan's overall profile wins.

11.9 AdjEM gap and Texas A&M's archetype (2012 Syracuse, Elite Eight) both point to advancement. Yale's twin (2022 Michigan, Sweet 16) had a stronger profile than this Yale team. The physicality mismatch is decisive.

Narrow 2.7 AdjEM edge for Ole Miss. Both archetypes are strong — Ole Miss matches 2018 Michigan (Runner-Up) and UNC matches 2011 Connecticut (Champion in some twins). Ole Miss's defensive edge (AdjD 97.0 vs 99.8) decides a close 6-11.

20.3 AdjEM gap is overwhelming. Iowa State's archetype (2013 Duke) lost in R64, which is a caution flag, but a 3-14 with this efficiency gap is a 95%+ favorite regardless. Lipscomb's twin (2013 Harvard) also lost in R64.

Only 2.6 AdjEM edge, but Marquette's champion archetype (2014 Connecticut, 0.963 similarity) is remarkable. That UConn team won the title from a similar efficiency profile. New Mexico's twin (2010 Baylor, Sweet 16) is solid but lower ceiling.

24.5 AdjEM gap. Michigan State's archetype (2010 Duke, Champion) reflects elite tournament DNA. Bryant's twin (2012 Norfolk State) pulled an upset, but that was a 15-over-2 — the stats don't support a repeat here against MSU's superior defense.
46.0 AdjEM gap — the largest in the tournament. Duke's archetype (2024 Purdue, Runner-Up) and AdjO of 130.6 suggest historically elite offense. Mount St. Mary's has no path.

Baylor's 1.8 AdjEM edge and archetype (2013 Syracuse, Final Four) give them the slight nod. Mississippi State's twin (2014 Connecticut, Champion) is impressive, but Baylor's offensive firepower (AdjO 121.3) and Scott Drew's tournament experience tilt this 8-9.

7.2 AdjEM advantage. Oregon's archetype (2014 Connecticut, Champion, 0.960) is extremely bullish. Liberty's twin (2016 Syracuse, Final Four) pulled an upset from the 10-seed line, not the 12. Oregon's efficiency edge holds.

20.9 AdjEM gap. Arizona's archetype (2016 Villanova, Champion, 0.934) suggests title-contender profile. Akron's twin (2012 Ohio, Sweet 16) was a 13-seed upset specialist, but Arizona's offensive firepower (AdjO 124.9) neutralizes that risk.

7.3 AdjEM edge driven by BYU's elite offense (AdjO 126.8). BYU's archetype (2024 Purdue, Runner-Up, 0.870) matches a team that reached the championship game. VCU's twin (2013 Syracuse, Final Four) has a strong profile but lower offensive output.

23.5 AdjEM gap. Wisconsin's archetype (2018 Michigan, Runner-Up, 0.931) matches a team that went deep. Montana's twin (2013 Harvard) was a first-round exit. Wisconsin's defensive profile (AdjD 96.8) suffocates mid-majors.

3.7 AdjEM edge with elite defense (AdjD 93.0 — 8 points better than Vanderbilt's 101.1). Saint Mary's archetype (2014 Wisconsin, Final Four) was similarly defense-first and slow-tempo. Vanderbilt's twin (2022 Iowa State, Sweet 16) is good but defense wins here.

29.0 AdjEM gap. Alabama's archetype (2012 North Carolina, Elite Eight) reflects high-powered offense that dominates in tournament settings. Robert Morris's twin (2012 Norfolk State) was a 15-seed upset specialist, but Alabama's AdjO 127.7 at 75.1 tempo overwhelms.
42.2 AdjEM gap with the nation's best defense (AdjD 87.3). Houston's archetype (2019 Virginia, Champion) won the title with a similarly suffocating defensive style. SIU Edwardsville has no path.

9.3 AdjEM gap is enormous for an 8-9 matchup — Gonzaga is massively underseeded. Gonzaga's archetype (2016 Villanova, Champion, 0.946) is a title-winner profile. Georgia's twin (2013 Syracuse, Final Four) is solid but Gonzaga's offensive arsenal (AdjO 124.8) is too much.

7.6 AdjEM edge. Clemson's archetype (2013 Syracuse, Final Four) suggests a team capable of a deep run. McNeese's twin (2016 Syracuse, Final Four) is intriguing for a 12-seed, but Clemson's defensive discipline (AdjD 95.5 at 64.6 tempo) grinds out mid-major challengers.

16.7 AdjEM gap. Purdue's archetype (2024 Purdue, Runner-Up) is literally the same program reaching back-to-back title games. High Point's twin (2023 Furman, R32) had an upset pedigree, but not against this level of offensive firepower (AdjO 125.6).

6.1 AdjEM edge. Illinois's archetype (2016 Oklahoma, Final Four) reflects a team that can make a deep run from this seed line. Xavier's twin (2021 UCLA, Final Four) is impressive but from a different seed line. Illinois's offense (AdjO 122.5) at a fast tempo controls this game.

18.7 AdjEM gap. Kentucky's archetype (2016 Oklahoma, Final Four, 0.931) and Troy's twin (2022 Saint Peter's, Elite Eight) create an interesting dynamic — Troy's archetype was a massive upset specialist. But the efficiency gap is too wide for a 14-seed.

7.8 AdjEM advantage. UCLA's archetype (2012 Syracuse, Elite Eight, 0.971) is a near-perfect match with a team that went deep. Utah State's twin (2022 Michigan, Sweet 16) suggests second-weekend capacity, but UCLA's defense (AdjD 94.3) is the difference.

26.2 AdjEM gap with elite defense (AdjD 91.9). Tennessee's archetype (2015 Wisconsin, Runner-Up, 0.979 — near perfect similarity) reached the championship game with the same defensive identity. Wofford has no path against this wall.

36.5 AdjEM gap. Florida's archetype (2017 Gonzaga, Runner-Up, 0.920) reached the title game with a similar offensive-juggernaut profile. Norfolk State's twin (2012 Norfolk State, R32) was a 15-seed upset team, not a 16-seed one. Florida cruises.

3.6 AdjEM edge. UConn's archetype (2013 Syracuse, Final Four) and back-to-back championship DNA give them a slight edge in this 8-9. Oklahoma's twin (2021 UCLA, Final Four, 0.927) is strong, but UConn's offensive efficiency (AdjO 120.8) in the half-court decides it.

Colorado State has a 5.6 AdjEM ADVANTAGE over the 5-seed Memphis (18.4 vs 12.8) — one of the biggest misseeds in the bracket. CSU's archetype (2013 Syracuse, Final Four, 0.937) matches a team that went deep. Memphis's twin (2017 South Carolina, Final Four) had a magic run but worse numbers. The data says upset.

18.8 AdjEM gap. Maryland's archetype (2011 Duke, Sweet 16, 0.986 — near-perfect) is a methodical 4-seed profile. Grand Canyon's twin (2014 Mercer, R32) had upset potential from the 14-line, not the 13. Maryland's defense (AdjD 92.1) is elite.

9.3 AdjEM edge. Missouri's archetype (2016 Oklahoma, Final Four) reflects a high-powered offensive team with deep run capability. Drake's twin (2010 Butler, Runner-Up) had incredible tournament magic, but Drake plays at 59.1 tempo — too slow to generate comeback possessions.

22.8 AdjEM gap. Texas Tech's archetype (2024 Purdue, Runner-Up, 0.924) is a near-match for a team that reached the championship. UNC Wilmington's twin (2013 Harvard, R64) lost in the first round. Texas Tech's offense (AdjO 126.1) is overwhelming.

4.1 AdjEM edge. Kansas's archetype (2010 West Virginia, Final Four, 0.947) and defensive profile (AdjD 93.7 vs 95.5) give them the edge. Arkansas's twin (2015 Michigan State, Final Four) has deep-run DNA, making this closer than the seed line suggests.

19.9 AdjEM gap with the tournament's best defense (AdjD 90.0). St. John's archetype (2010 West Virginia, Final Four) matches a team that made a deep run. Omaha's twin (2013 Harvard, R64) was a first-round exit. This is a blowout.

round_of_32.reasoning: Auburn's AdjEM (34.7) is 15 points above Creighton's (19.7). Despite Creighton's champion archetype (2014 UConn), the efficiency gap is too wide. Auburn's archetype (2024 Purdue, Runner-Up) confirms a deep-run profile. Auburn's defense (AdjD 93.0) contains Creighton's motion offense.

Near-identical AdjEM (21.8 each) makes this a true toss-up. Texas A&M's archetype (2012 Syracuse, Elite Eight) edges Michigan's (2014 Michigan State, Final Four) in this specific matchup context. Texas A&M's defensive edge (AdjD 94.2 vs 93.9) and SEC physicality decide it.

Iowa State's AdjEM (26.0) is 3.8 points above Ole Miss (22.2). Iowa State's archetype twin (2013 Duke) lost in R64, which is a caution flag, but their defensive profile (AdjD 93.3 vs 97.0) is too strong. Iowa State grinds Ole Miss down.

Michigan State's AdjEM (24.9) holds a 5.5-point edge over Marquette (19.4). MSU's archetype (2010 Duke, Champion) reflects tournament-winning DNA. Despite Marquette's champion twin (2014 UConn), Michigan State's superior defense (AdjD 92.3) and Izzo's tournament pedigree prevail.

Duke's AdjEM (38.5) towers 17.5 points over Baylor (21.0). Duke's archetype (2024 Purdue, Runner-Up) and generational offense (AdjO 130.6) make this a dominant win. Baylor's Final Four archetype (2013 Syracuse) can't overcome this talent gap.

Arizona's 7.3-point AdjEM edge (27.5 vs 20.2) is decisive. Arizona's champion archetype (2016 Villanova, 0.934 similarity) suggests a team built for deep tournament runs. Oregon's champion twin (2014 UConn) is strong but Arizona's offensive firepower (AdjO 124.9) overwhelms in this Pac-12 matchup.

BYU (AdjEM 26.6) slightly edges Wisconsin (26.4) by efficiency alone, but Wisconsin's deliberate tempo (67.9) negates BYU's explosive offense (AdjO 126.8). Wisconsin's archetype (2018 Michigan, Runner-Up) and superior defense (AdjD 96.8 vs 100.2) win this grinding contest.

Alabama's AdjEM (31.1) is 9.9 points above Saint Mary's (21.2). Alabama's pace (75.1 tempo) forces Saint Mary's (61.9 tempo) into an uncomfortable speed. Saint Mary's archetype (2014 Wisconsin, Final Four) thrived at slow pace — Alabama ensures they never get it.

Houston's AdjEM (37.3) vs Gonzaga's (28.1) — a 9.2-point edge. Houston's champion archetype (2019 Virginia) won a title with the same defensive suffocation (AdjD 87.3). Gonzaga's champion twin (2016 Villanova) had elite offense, but Houston's defense is historically elite. Defense wins in March.

Purdue's AdjEM (26.2) edges Clemson's (21.8) by 4.4 points. Purdue's archetype (2024 Purdue, Runner-Up — literally themselves from last year) shows they know how to navigate the tournament. Purdue's offense (AdjO 125.6 vs 117.3) creates a decisive scoring gap.

Close match (AdjEM 24.9 vs 24.0). Kentucky's archetype (2016 Oklahoma, Final Four) and Illinois's twin (2016 Oklahoma, Final Four — same team!) show remarkably similar profiles. Kentucky's higher offensive output (AdjO 123.4 vs 122.5) and roster pedigree tip a coin-flip.

Tennessee's AdjEM (28.3) beats UCLA's (23.6) by 4.7 points. Tennessee's archetype (2015 Wisconsin, Runner-Up, 0.979 — near-perfect match) reached the championship game with this exact defensive profile (AdjD 91.9). UCLA's twin (2012 Syracuse, Elite Eight) can't match Tennessee's defensive intensity.

Florida's AdjEM (33.6) is 14 points above UConn's (19.6). Florida's archetype (2017 Gonzaga, Runner-Up) reached the title game with a similar offensive juggernaut profile (AdjO 127.8). Even UConn's championship pedigree can't overcome this statistical chasm.

Maryland's AdjEM (26.5) is 8.1 points above Colorado State's (18.4). Maryland's archetype (2011 Duke, Sweet 16, 0.986 near-perfect) is a methodical team that grinds out wins. Despite Colorado State's first-round upset momentum, Maryland's defense (AdjD 92.1) suffocates the Rams.

Texas Tech's AdjEM (28.5) holds a 3.8-point edge over Missouri (24.7). Both have strong archetypes — Texas Tech (2024 Purdue, Runner-Up) and Missouri (2016 Oklahoma, Final Four). Texas Tech's balanced profile (AdjO 126.1, AdjD 97.6) wins this clash of high-powered offenses.

St. John's defensive wall (AdjD 90.0 — best in bracket) is the difference despite only a 0.6 AdjEM edge (22.9 vs 22.3). Both archetypes are strong (Final Four twins), but St. John's defense creates the kind of grind that eliminates Kansas's offensive rhythm.

sweet_16.reasoning: Auburn's AdjEM (34.7) dominates Texas A&M's (21.8) by 12.9 points. Both SEC teams, but Auburn's archetype (2024 Purdue, Runner-Up) and elite offense (AdjO 127.7 vs 116.0) create a decisive gap. How they got here: Auburn handled Creighton; TAMU edged Michigan. Auburn's defense (AdjD 93.0) contains TAMU's guards.

Razor-thin AdjEM edge for Iowa State (26.0 vs 24.9). Michigan State's archetype (2010 Duke, Champion) edges Iowa State's twin (2013 Duke, R64 exit). Izzo's tournament pedigree and MSU's superior defense (AdjD 92.3 vs 93.3) tip this coin-flip. MSU has been through Iowa State and Marquette to get here — battle-tested.

Duke's AdjEM (38.5) is 11 points above Arizona's (27.5). Duke's archetype (2024 Purdue, Runner-Up) and Arizona's champion twin (2016 Villanova) make this a clash of elite profiles, but Duke's offensive superiority (AdjO 130.6 vs 124.9) and defensive edge (AdjD 92.0 vs 97.4) are decisive.

Alabama's AdjEM (31.1) is 4.7 points above Wisconsin's (26.4). Wisconsin's deliberate tempo (67.9) will slow Alabama's transition, but Alabama's offensive efficiency (AdjO 127.7 vs 123.1) means they score effectively in half-court too. Alabama's archetype (2012 UNC, Elite Eight) has the athleticism to overwhelm Wisconsin's system.

Houston's AdjEM (37.3) towers over Purdue's (26.2). Houston's champion archetype (2019 Virginia) won the title with the same suffocating defense. Purdue's archetype (2024 Purdue, Runner-Up) shows they can reach this stage, but Houston's defense (AdjD 87.3) holds Purdue's offense (AdjO 125.6) well below season average.

Tennessee's AdjEM (28.3) edges Kentucky's (24.9) by 3.4 points. Tennessee's archetype (2015 Wisconsin, Runner-Up, 0.979 near-perfect match) reached the championship with this exact defensive identity. In an SEC rivalry, Tennessee's defense (AdjD 91.9 vs 98.5 — a 6.6-point gap) is the decisive factor.

Florida's AdjEM (33.6) is 7.1 points above Maryland (26.5). Both have elite defenses (AdjD 94.2 vs 92.1), but Florida's offense (AdjO 127.8 vs 118.6) creates decisive separation. Florida's archetype (2017 Gonzaga, Runner-Up) confirms a title-game trajectory. Florida advances comfortably.

Texas Tech's AdjEM (28.5) is 5.6 points above St. John's (22.9). Despite St. John's having the bracket's best defense (AdjD 90.0), Texas Tech's offense (AdjO 126.1 vs 113.0) creates a 13.1-point offensive gap that defense alone can't close. Texas Tech's archetype (2024 Purdue, Runner-Up) confirms deep-run capacity.

elite_8.reasoning: Auburn's AdjEM (34.7) is 9.8 points above Michigan State's (24.9). Both have champion-level archetypes — Auburn (2024 Purdue, Runner-Up) and MSU (2010 Duke, Champion). But Auburn's offensive firepower (AdjO 127.7 vs 117.3) and the efficiency gap are too much even for Izzo's tournament magic. Auburn's path: Alabama State, Creighton, Texas A&M — progressively tougher opponents handled with ease.

Duke (AdjEM 38.5) holds a 7.4-point edge over Alabama (31.1). Both are offensive powerhouses (AdjO 130.6 vs 127.7), but Duke's defense (AdjD 92.0 vs 96.6) is the differentiator. Duke's archetype (2024 Purdue, Runner-Up) suggests a championship-game ceiling. Alabama's archetype (2012 UNC, Elite Eight) suggests this is their wall. Duke's balanced excellence wins.

Houston (AdjEM 37.3) vs Tennessee (28.3) — both elite defensive teams (AdjD 87.3 vs 91.9). Houston's champion archetype (2019 Virginia) won a title with this exact defensive suffocation. Tennessee's near-perfect twin (2015 Wisconsin, Runner-Up) lost in the championship. Houston's 9-point AdjEM edge and the nation's best defense make the difference in a low-scoring grinder.

Florida's AdjEM (33.6) is 5.1 points above Texas Tech (28.5). Florida's archetype (2017 Gonzaga, Runner-Up) reached the title game with a similar offensive juggernaut profile. Texas Tech's archetype (2024 Purdue, Runner-Up) also reached the title game, but Florida's offensive edge (AdjO 127.8 vs 126.1) and superior defense (AdjD 94.2 vs 97.6) combine for the win.

final_four.reasoning: Auburn (AdjEM 34.7) vs Florida (33.6) — only 1.1-point gap. Florida's archetype (2017 Gonzaga, Runner-Up) reached the championship game. Auburn's twin (2024 Purdue, Runner-Up) also reached the final. Both are 1-seeds with elite offense. The difference: Florida's path (Norfolk State, UConn, Maryland, Texas Tech) was tougher in later rounds, and Florida's offense (AdjO 127.8 vs 127.7) matches Auburn's. Florida's slightly faster tempo (69.9 vs 68.3) generates more opportunities in a high-stakes game.

Houston (AdjEM 37.3) vs Duke (38.5) — Duke has the slight edge. But Houston's champion archetype (2019 Virginia) won the title with defensive dominance. Duke's twin (2024 Purdue, Runner-Up) lost in the final. Houston's defense (AdjD 87.3) is historically elite and 4.7 points better than Duke's (92.0). In a Final Four where Duke must crack the nation's best defense, Houston's wall holds. The x-factor: Houston holds opponents to season lows at this stage.

championship.reasoning: Florida (AdjEM 33.6) vs Houston (37.3) — Houston has the statistical edge, but Florida's archetype (2017 Gonzaga, Runner-Up) and offensive firepower (AdjO 127.8) create enough volume to crack Houston's historic defense. Houston's champion twin (2019 Virginia) won by surviving close games — but Florida's faster tempo (69.9 vs 61.8) generates more possessions, limiting Houston's ability to control pace. Florida's path through tougher matchups (UConn, Maryland, Texas Tech, Auburn) has battle-tested this team. In a championship game, Florida's offensive depth finds enough cracks in Houston's wall to pull the upset.
